faculty.summary: Beth Harrison, a surgical pathologist with expertise in breast and gynecologic pathology, has joined the Department of Pathology at Harvard Medical School as part-time faculty and an Assistant Professor.
faculty.name: Beth Harrison
faculty.why_it_matters: New hires need to outfit labs with supplies and equipment.

faculty.summary: Shelby Hutcherson, a board-certified clinical chemist, has joined the Department of Pathology at Harvard Medical School as Assistant Director of Clinical Chemistry and Director of Immunology in the Division of Clinical Laboratory Medicine.
faculty.name: Shelby Hutcherson
faculty.department: Pathology, Division of Clinical Laboratory Medicine, Harvard Medical School
faculty.why_it_matters: New hires need to outfit labs with supplies and equipment.

faculty.summary: Jia-Chi “Jack” Wang, a board-certified clinical cytogeneticist and molecular geneticist, has joined the Department of Pathology at Harvard Medical School as Director of Clinical Cytogenetics, Reproductive and Perinatal Diagnostics, and Director of Molecular Pathology Quality Assurance in the Division of Clinical Laboratory Medicine.
faculty.name: Jia-Chi “Jack” Wang
faculty.department: Pathology, Division of Clinical Laboratory Medicine, Harvard Medical School
faculty.why_it_matters: New hires need to outfit labs with supplies and equipment.

project.summary: Harvard University announced a three-year, $1.05 million grant to the Association of Historically Black Colleges and Universities Research Institutions (AHRI) through its Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery (H&LS) Initiative. The grant aims to enhance research infrastructure and provide technical assistance to 15 HBCUs to boost their collective research, innovation, and impact, helping them achieve R1 status. Harvard's Office of the Vice Provost for Research (OVPR) and Office for Sponsored Programs (OSP) will provide technical support.
project.project_name: Grant to the Association of Historically Black Colleges and Universities Research Institutions (AHRI) through Harvard & the Legacy of Slavery (H&LS) Initiative
project.scope: Supports research infrastructure and technical assistance at 15 HBCUs to enhance collective research, innovation, and impact, aiming for R1 status. Harvard's OVPR and OSP will provide technical support.
project.why_it_matters: This multi-year, multi-million dollar grant will directly increase the research capacity and infrastructure at 15 HBCUs, leading to increased demand for a wide range of lab supplies, reagents, consumables, and scientific equipment at these institutions.

project.summary: Harvard University has expanded its research alliance agreement with Northpond Labs, the R&D affiliate of Northpond Ventures, to advance healthcare innovations. Northpond Labs will provide significant funding for research across Harvard's Wyss Institute, Harvard Medical School (HMS), John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS), and Faculty of Arts and Sciences (FAS). This expansion builds on a successful 2020 alliance that created the Lab for Bioengineering Research and Innovation at the Wyss Institute.
project.project_name: Expanded Research Alliance with Northpond Labs to Advance Solutions in Healthcare
project.scope: Translational research, accelerating commercialization, and advancing breakthrough healthcare innovations across Harvard labs at the Wyss Institute, Harvard Medical School, John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences, and Faculty of Arts and Sciences. Focus areas include synthetic biology, cell therapy, drug discovery, and protein engineering.
project.timeline: Ongoing, building on an alliance established in 2020
project.why_it_matters: This expanded research alliance across multiple scientific and engineering departments will drive demand for advanced scientific equipment, specialized reagents, and a broad range of lab consumables for healthcare innovation and therapeutic development.

project.summary: The Office of the Vice Provost for Research (OVPR) at Harvard University announced awards to five projects from the Harvard University Genomic Medicines Fund. This fund supports research involving genome editing that is useful or relevant to the field of genomic medicine for the prevention or treatment of human disease. Individual awards are for two years with a maximum of $200K per year ($400K total) in direct costs per project, collectively exceeding $1M.
project.project_name: Harvard University Genomic Medicines Fund Projects
project.scope: Research involving genome editing for the prevention or treatment of human disease, including targeted gene correction and repair of muscle cells toward a treatment for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, and uncovering molecular mechanisms that drive stem cell aging in skin. Projects involve faculty from Harvard Medical School, Wyss Institute, and Faculty of Arts and Sciences.
project.why_it_matters: Funding for five new genomic medicine projects will create demand for specialized reagents, gene editing tools (e.g., CRISPR kits), sequencing equipment, cell culture supplies, and advanced microscopy.

project.summary: Heidelberg University and Harvard University have begun implementing the first joint research projects under their strategic partnership, which was formally expanded in August 2025. The state of Baden-Württemberg supports this cooperation with a total of ten million euros in funding. 23 joint initiatives have been selected for funding, with project funding starting on April 1, 2026.
project.scope: Deepen established cooperation arrangements through joint research, build transatlantic networks, and drive forward projects with great scientific momentum across a wide range of disciplines, with a particular focus on Medicine (9 successful applications). The overall partnership is supported by ten million euros from the state of Baden-Württemberg.
project.timeline: Projects planned to run for up to 24 months; project funding started April 1, 2026
project.why_it_matters: This large-scale international research partnership, with a significant focus on medicine, will generate demand for a diverse array of laboratory equipment, reagents, and consumables across multiple scientific disciplines at Harvard.

ai_summary: Harvard University is significantly expanding its research capacity. Recent faculty hires in Pathology, Clinical Chemistry, and Molecular Genetics at Harvard Medical School, combined with over $12 million in new funding for genomic medicine, healthcare innovation, and joint research projects, indicate a substantial increase in lab activity. This growth creates significant opportunities for lab supply sales teams to support diverse and expanding research needs across multiple departments.
